steps.title: Map the structure around the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: "improvements in efficiency often correlate negatively with resource ___" is a complete independent clause ending with the noun "conservation." After the blank: "efficiency gains, lowering the cost of use, may increase demand to the extent that resource consumption ultimately rises" is also complete. The word "though" signals a contrast between the two ideas.

steps.title: Pick the punctuation that handles both clauses
steps.content: "Though" works as a subordinating conjunction meaning "although." When "though" opens a subordinate clause that follows an independent clause, the pattern is IC, though SC — a single comma before "though" is all that is required. That gives: "...resource conservation, though efficiency gains... may increase demand..."

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices by the rule each violates
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'conservation, though,': the comma after "though" turns it into a parenthetical adverb, leaving two independent clauses spliced together by commas. Comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'conservation; though': a space before a semicolon is non-standard, and the semicolon followed by "though" with no comma garbles the join. Punctuation error. ✗
  • Choice C — 'conservation, though;': places a semicolon after "though" with a leading space and only one comma around the parenthetical "though," breaking the IC;IC pattern. Misplaced semicolon. ✗
  • Choice D — 'conservation, though': treats "though" as a subordinator opening the next clause, producing the clean IC, though SC structure. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
